This document has been updated to include AKS on bare metal as a supported distribution and to remove the preview label from Arc-enabled clusters. These updates enhance the clarity and comprehensiveness of the supported environments for Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager.

The documentation now includes support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) on the Application Gateway for Containers association subnet for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, allowing users to configure both inbound and outbound rules. Additionally, users can implement User Defined Routes (UDRs) for fine-grain control over routing, including scenarios to disable BGP route propagation and direct traffic to the internet, enhancing traffic management capabilities.

The documentation now reflects that Fleet Manager supports joining AKS and Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters as member clusters without the preview designation, indicating that this feature is now generally available. Users can confidently utilize Fleet Manager to manage their existing AKS and Arc-enabled clusters without concerns about preview limitations.

The documentation now states that Fleet Manager can support joining up to 1,000 Kubernetes clusters, an increase from the previous limit of 500. Additionally, it clarifies that Fleet Manager supports both Azure-hosted AKS clusters and Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters as member clusters, removing the previous mention of Arc-enabled cluster support being in preview.

The documentation now includes updates for version 1.10.28, which introduces security updates, and version 1.10.27, which provides a hotfix for a pod crash related to GitHub issue 5758. Additionally, the release notes for version 1.0.0 highlight the General Availability of the Gateway API, including a URL redirect for both Gateway and Ingress API, and breaking changes regarding the TLS Policy for Gateway API and the renaming of the Listener to SectionName. Users should review these changes to understand the implications for their configurations and deployments.

The documentation now specifies that users can create an AKS cluster with a GPU-enabled default node pool using newly added GPU types, including NVIDIA L-series GPUs and NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada GPUs. Additionally, the commands for creating the cluster and adding a node pool have been updated to use the placeholder `<GPU_ENABLED_VM_SIZE>` instead of the previous `<Standard_NC16_L4_1>`, allowing for more flexibility in selecting GPU sizes.

The documentation now clarifies that Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager's support for Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters has been removed from preview status, indicating that users can expect more stable functionality. Additionally, the capabilities table has been updated to reflect that cross-cluster networking is now in preview for AKS clusters, while Arc-enabled clusters remain unsupported in this area. Users should also note that Fleet Manager support for Arc-enabled clusters is limited to Azure public cloud regions due to dependencies on Azure Arc Gateway.

The documentation has been updated to reflect the latest version of the application gateway for containers, changing the version parameter from 1.10.21 and 1.10.27 to 1.10.28 in multiple command examples. Users can now utilize the updated version, which may include new features or fixes not present in the previous versions. This change ensures that users are following the most current practices when deploying the application gateway.

The documentation now includes information about the migration of Azure Monitor services, such as Container Insights and Managed Prometheus, to a cluster extension-based backend model. This change is nondisruptive, ensuring that users will not experience any changes in functionality or user experience, and all existing tools like Azure CLI and the Azure portal will continue to operate as expected. Additionally, users are informed about the Container Network Insights Agent, which can be utilized for diagnosing and troubleshooting networking issues in AKS clusters.

The documentation for creating an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster on bare metal using an ARM template has been updated to improve clarity on control plane IP conflict resolution, now advising users to select an IP within the same subnet as the edge machine IP. Additionally, the parameters and commands for AKS bare metal cluster creation have been refactored, allowing for clearer configuration of the `edgeMachineName`, `controlPlaneIp`, `adminGroupObjectIds`, and `sshPublicKey`. The documentation now includes a new section on enabling LocalDNS for improved DNS resolution reliability in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). Users are advised to enable LocalDNS on their node pools to enhance service connectivity during transient DNS outages, as it reduces reliance on centralized CoreDNS pods and mitigates issues like dropped DNS queries in high-throughput environments. Additionally, LocalDNS can serve stale cached responses when upstream DNS is unavailable, ensuring continued pod connectivity during disruptions.

The documentation for eBPF Host Routing has been updated to clarify that enabling this feature updates nodes through rolling upgrades for node pools, ensuring existing connections are respected during node drain timeout. Additionally, it now specifies that eBPF Host Routing cannot be used with Static Egress Gateway and that Istio Ambient through a self-managed installation with ACNS Performance mode is not supported. Users should also note that dual-stack connectivity is supported, but compatibility is limited to specific OS versions.

This article provides guidance on updating existing Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters to use newer IP address management (IPAM) modes and data plane technologies, specifically Azure CNI Overlay and Azure CNI Powered by Cilium. It clarifies the migration paths and the implications of updating the data plane, including compatibility notes for Windows node pools and node auto-provisioning.

Additionally, the supported operating systems section has been revised to specify that Ubuntu 22.04 is not supported for CVMs, while Ubuntu 20.04 and 24.04 are supported for specific Kubernetes versions. Users are also informed that Azure Container Linux does not support CVM node pools.

The documentation now specifies that new AKS Automatic clusters will by default enable managed system node pools and LocalDNS, and users cannot create AKS Automatic clusters without managed system node pools in any region. Additionally, it is now required to use Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or later, and several extensions, including Dapr and Azure Machine Learning, are no longer supported. Users can implement UDRs to disable Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) route propagation or to direct all traffic (0.0.0.0/0) directly to the internet, which helps prevent issues with client return traffic. Additionally, it clarifies that `--os-sku Ubuntu2204` is for rolling back to Ubuntu 22.04, and users must switch to a supported OS SKU to upgrade their Kubernetes version to 1.37 or higher.

The documentation now includes the general availability of Azure Container Linux (ACL) as an OS option for AKS starting with version 1.34, allowing users to deploy ACL node pools in new AKS clusters, add them to existing clusters, and migrate existing Linux node pools to ACL. Additionally, two new optimized Linux OS options, Azure Linux OS Guard and Flatcar Container Linux for AKS, are introduced, providing enhanced security features and specialized configurations for containerized workloads. Users are guided on selecting the appropriate OS based on their needs, with recommendations for each option.

The quickstart guide for deploying the Application Gateway for Containers AKS add-on has been updated to clarify that the add-on is not supported on AKS Automatic clusters. It also includes details on validating the GatewayClass condition.

The documentation for AKS Automatic Clusters with Managed System Node Pools has been updated to clarify that managed system node pools are now enabled by default for new automatic clusters, allowing users to focus on applications while AKS manages the underlying infrastructure. Additionally, the documentation highlights that the cluster autoscaler and node auto-repair features are enabled for system nodes in the managed system node pool, enhancing performance and reliability. Users are advised to recreate existing automatic clusters without managed system node pools to take advantage of these features.

The documentation now includes a new troubleshooting section that addresses issues related to member clusters failing to connect and the `clustermesh-apiserver` LoadBalancer Service not receiving an external IP. Users are guided on how to check the service status, identify potential authorization errors, and manually assign the necessary **Network Contributor** role to the AKS cluster identity to resolve these issues. Additionally, the instructions for creating AKS clusters have been clarified to emphasize the use of a flat network and the importance of joining them to a Fleet after creation.

The documentation now clarifies that when using the Azure portal to add member clusters, users must have Contributor permissions for the resource group where Fleet Manager is deployed. Additionally, it emphasizes that certain RBAC roles are not supported for Arc-enabled member clusters, and access control for specific Kubernetes Custom Resources (CRs) is also not supported for these Azure RBAC roles.

The documentation for upgrading AKS node pools has been updated to clarify that the `maxSurge` parameter can now be set as either an integer or a percentage. Additionally, the default value for `maxUnavailable` is now specified as `0`, which requires `maxSurge` to be greater than `0`. A new fallback strategy during upgrades has been introduced, allowing AKS to attempt a surge of 1 node if the full surge isn't possible due to quota or capacity limitations, ensuring that upgrades can proceed even under constrained conditions.

The documentation for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) pricing tiers has been updated to clarify the features and recommendations for the Free, Standard, and Premium tiers. Users can now see that the Free tier supports clusters with up to 1,000 nodes, while the Standard tier is the default for Automatic SKU clusters and supports up to 5,000 nodes with an enabled uptime SLA by default. Additionally, the Premium tier now explicitly mentions support for workloads requiring 24-month Long Term Support (LTS) Kubernetes version support.

A new quickstart guide has been added for deploying an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster using Azure Container Linux (ACL) via the Azure CLI. This guide includes prerequisites, step-by-step instructions, and a sample application deployment, making it easier for users to get started with ACL.

The documentation for the Kubernetes Gateway API Ingress for Istio Service Mesh Add-on for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to remove all preview tags, indicating that the feature is now generally available. Users can now configure Istio ingress without the previous preview limitations, reflecting its stable release status.

A new article has been introduced detailing the process of migrating existing AKS node pools to Azure Container Linux (ACL). It outlines two methods for migration, prerequisites, and limitations, providing essential guidance for users looking to transition to ACL.

The documentation now specifies that users must have Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or later to utilize the features described. Additionally, the output after deployment now includes a `hostedSystemProfile` property indicating whether it is enabled, which users can verify using the `az aks show` command. The resource definition has also been updated to reflect the new API version `2026-02-01`.
[!IMPORTANT]
This document has been updated with new metadata and author information, reflecting recent changes and ensuring that the documentation is current and accurate.

The integration guide for SIEM with Microsoft Sentinel has been updated to reflect the latest steps for enabling the Microsoft Sentinel workspace, enhancing security monitoring capabilities for Application Gateway for Containers.

The documentation for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to emphasize best practices for cost optimization, including the introduction of AKS Automatic, which preconfigures cost-saving features such as node auto-provisioning, workload autoscaling, and managed Prometheus. Users can now leverage AKS Automatic to reduce engineering effort and operational overhead, ensuring that resources are allocated efficiently without manual configuration. Additionally, the article provides detailed insights on leveraging Azure discounts, strategic infrastructure selection, and dynamic rightsizing to further optimize costs.

The quickstart guide for creating an AKS Automatic cluster in a custom virtual network has been updated to reflect the latest features and best practices. It emphasizes the ease of use and the benefits of AKS Automatic for developers and platform engineers.
[!IMPORTANT]
Learn how to quickly deploy an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster with Azure Container Linux (ACL) for AKS using an Azure Resource Manager (ARM) template.

The limitations section for AKS Automatic clusters has been revised to clarify the requirements and constraints for deploying in various Azure regions. This update ensures users are well-informed about the necessary conditions for successful deployment.

The overview of Azure Container Linux (ACL) has been enhanced to provide a comprehensive understanding of its features, benefits, and integration with Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). It emphasizes ACL's immutable design and its alignment with Azure's security and compliance requirements, marking its general availability.

Step-by-step guide to migrate ingress traffic from the application routing add-on with ingress-nginx to the application routing Gateway API (Istio) implementation on Azure Kubernetes Service with zero downtime.

The documentation now includes a new section on the Kubernetes gateway, providing users with updated guidance on its integration with AKS Automatic. This addition enhances the existing content by offering detailed instructions and best practices for utilizing the Kubernetes gateway feature effectively. Users can expect improved clarity and support for implementing this functionality in their AKS environments.

The documentation for using Confidential Virtual Machines (CVMs) in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to clarify the security features and benefits of CVMs. It now includes details on the types of workloads that may require CVMs and highlights the support for both AMD and Intel-based CVMs, enhancing the understanding of their application in sensitive environments.

The documentation now specifies that users must have Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or later installed to create AKS clusters. Additionally, system pools must contain at least two nodes, and the OS SKU options for single node pools have been expanded to include `AzureContainerLinux`. Users can now create clusters with a single ACL node pool using the `--os-sku AzureContainerLinux` parameter, and the documentation clarifies that all node pools must reside in the same virtual network.

The documentation has been updated to reflect the new Helm chart version 1.10.28, replacing the previous version 1.10.27 in multiple command examples. Additionally, the instructions for overriding namespaces during the `helm install` command have been clarified, emphasizing that users can now specify the `--namespace` parameter and the `--set albController.namespace` property more clearly. The section numbering for uninstalling the ALB Controller has also been corrected, improving the overall clarity of the instructions.

The documentation now emphasizes that Application Gateway for Containers has a migration utility to assist users in automatically translating AGIC Ingress to Gateway API, providing a more streamlined migration process. Additionally, the language has been updated for clarity, indicating that users can retire the Application Gateway Ingress Controller configuration after validating the Application Gateway for Containers functionality. Users should also be aware that certain dependencies on the Ingress Controller may not be available in the new service, which could affect their migration strategy.

The documentation now specifies that Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or later is required, updating from the previous version of 2.77.0. Additionally, the text has been clarified in several places, such as changing "reserves at least 9 IPs" to "reserves at least nine (9) IPs," and modifying phrases like "log in" to "sign in" for consistency. These changes enhance clarity and ensure users are aware of the updated CLI requirements.

This document introduces various load balancing strategies available in Application Gateway for Containers, emphasizing their importance in optimizing performance and enhancing reliability in dynamic environments. It details algorithms like Round Robin, Weighted Round Robin, and Slow Start, along with their configurations and scenarios.

The documentation for eBPF Host Routing has been updated to clarify that it is incompatible with nodes running operating systems other than Ubuntu 24.04 or Azure Linux 3.0, and it is not supported with Confidential VMs and Pod Sandboxing. Additionally, users should note that eBPF Host Routing cannot be used with the Static Egress Gateway, and Istio Ambient through a self-managed installation with ACNS Performance mode is also not supported.

The documentation now specifies that users must use `azure-cli` version `2.86.0` or higher to work with the Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) application routing add-on. Additionally, the section on configuring ingress with the Kubernetes Gateway API has been updated to remove the preview designation, indicating that the feature is now generally available. Users are also informed that self-managed Gateway API CRDs are unsupported with the application routing add-on.

The documentation now includes detailed guidance on using User Defined Routes (UDRs) for the Application Gateway for Containers, allowing users to manage routing more effectively between the gateway and AKS clusters. Users can disable Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) route propagation and create UDRs to direct traffic to the internet, which helps prevent issues with client return traffic. Additionally, Network Security Groups (NSGs) are fully supported for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, enabling more robust inbound and outbound traffic management.

The documentation now includes details about new flexible load balancing strategies available with Application Gateway for Containers, such as Least Request, Load Aware Routing, Ring Hash, Round Robin, and Weighted Round Robin. Users can leverage these strategies to optimize traffic management and improve application performance. Additionally, the section on traffic splitting has been updated to reflect the inclusion of weighted round robin capabilities.

The documentation now specifies that users must use Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or higher to work with the Managed Gateway API. Additionally, the installation instructions have been updated to clarify that the `aks-preview` extension must be at version 19.0.0b4 or later, ensuring users have the correct tools for installation and configuration.

The documentation now includes a new section on Azure Container Linux (ACL) node images, detailing their validation by AKS and the use of Azure Linux packages with Flatcar tooling. Users can now find specific use cases for ACL node images, including options for AMD64 and ARM64 VM sizes that utilize Trusted Launch. Additionally, the versioning format for both Azure Linux with OS Guard and Flatcar Container Linux node images has been clarified to follow the AKS date-based format.

This document has been revised to provide clearer instructions on enabling eBPF Host Routing with Advanced Container Networking Services. It emphasizes the importance of registering the feature flag and includes updated commands for better clarity.

The documentation now includes a detailed Service Level Agreement (SLA) for AKS Automatic clusters, which guarantees 99.9% pod readiness operations within 5 minutes and 99.95% availability of the Kubernetes API server. Users can benefit from this SLA without any additional configuration, as it is automatically included with every AKS Automatic cluster. Additionally, the ingress configuration has been updated to include the Kubernetes Gateway API for new clusters starting from AKS version 1.36, enhancing the flexibility of ingress management.

The API specification for Kubernetes has been updated to include the LoadAwareConfig, which defines configurations for load-aware routing, enhancing the management of traffic distribution based on backend server utilization.

The documentation now includes updated version information for the ALB Controller Service Mesh Extension, reflecting the new version 1.10.28. Additionally, it clarifies that the integration assumes an Istio sidecar-based service mesh, as ambient mode is not supported, and provides specific instructions for configuring sidecar injection based on the Istio version installed. Users can now also see examples updated to use the namespace "test-infra" instead of "istio-example-namespace," ensuring consistency in the provided commands and configurations.

The cost optimization best practices article has been significantly updated to provide new recommendations and best practices for optimizing costs in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), including the use of AKS Automatic.

The documentation now specifies that users must use Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or higher to install and manage the Managed Gateway API on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). Additionally, the installation instructions have been updated to remove references to the preview status of the Managed Gateway API, indicating that it is now generally available. Users can also find updated commands for installing and updating the `aks-preview` extension.

The Istio Gateway API documentation has been updated to remove preview tags, indicating that the feature is now generally available. This change reflects the maturity of the feature and provides users with the latest configuration instructions.

The application routing add-on documentation has been updated to reflect the latest changes in the Kubernetes Gateway API. This includes removing preview tags and updating the installation instructions, ensuring users have access to the most current information.

Step-by-step guide to migrate ingress traffic from the application routing add-on with ingress-nginx to the application routing Gateway API (Istio) implementation on Azure Kubernetes Service with zero downtime.

Updates include the addition of user-defined routes (UDRs) for fine-grain control over the Application Gateway for Containers' association subnet. The documentation now warns about potential asymmetrical routing issues and provides scenarios for configuring UDRs to ensure proper traffic flow.

The documentation has been updated to clarify the behavior of the maxSurge and maxUnavailable settings during upgrades. It introduces a fallback strategy for upgrades, ensuring that even under capacity constraints, upgrades can proceed smoothly. This includes new commands and explanations for setting these parameters.

The documentation for configuring cross-cluster networking in Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager has been updated to include a troubleshooting section and additional commands for managing member clusters. Key changes include clarifications on the role assignments needed for the AKS cluster identity and detailed steps for creating and applying network profiles.

The documentation for cross-cluster networking in Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager has been updated to include a new troubleshooting section, clarifications on cluster creation prerequisites, and a clean-up resources section. Additionally, there are improvements in command usage and examples for managing Cilium multi-cluster networking.

Updated the quickstart guide for creating a fleet and members portal in Azure Kubernetes Service. Added a permissions reference for Azure RBAC Contributor, enhancing clarity on required roles for users.

This update adds a note regarding Azure Portal permissions for adding member clusters, emphasizing that users need Contributor permissions for the resource group where Fleet Manager is deployed. It also clarifies that certain RBAC roles are not supported for Arc-enabled member clusters and specific Kubernetes Custom Resources.

This document provides detailed instructions on configuring cross-cluster networking for Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager, including the use of managed Cilium multi-cluster networking. It includes a new section on cleaning up resources and troubleshooting, enhancing the overall usability and clarity of the documentation.

The documentation now includes detailed guidance on using User Defined Routes (UDRs) for the Application Gateway for Containers, allowing users to manage routing more effectively between the gateway and AKS clusters. Users can disable Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) route propagation and create UDRs to direct traffic to the internet, which helps prevent issues with client return traffic. Additionally, Network Security Groups (NSGs) are fully supported for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, enabling more granular control over inbound and outbound traffic rules.

The documentation now includes a tip highlighting that users who do not require full control of the NVIDIA GPU Operator can utilize AKS-managed GPU node pools, which automatically install and maintain the NVIDIA driver, Kubernetes device plugin, and DCGM metrics exporter. Additionally, a comparison section has been added to clarify the benefits of using AKS-managed GPU node pools.

The documentation now clarifies that users can opt into Long-Term Support (LTS) for their AKS clusters while still in community support without incurring additional Premium-tier charges until the LTS support window begins. It also specifies that LTS Premium-tier billing starts only after the Kubernetes minor version exits community support, ensuring users are not charged prematurely. Additionally, the examples and commands have been updated for clarity, including the transition to Premium-tier billing and the process for disabling LTS.
Modified the link for creating a free Azure account to direct users to a more appropriate page, improving clarity for new users.

The documentation now clarifies that the health monitoring feature for GPU node pools specifically applies to AKS-managed GPU node pools, enhancing user understanding of the scope of the Node Problem Detector (NPD) functionality. Additionally, minor wording adjustments were made for consistency, ensuring that users are aware that NPD monitors the health of the NVIDIA device plugin service in these environments.
Modified the link for creating a free Azure account to ensure users are directed to the correct resource, improving user experience.
Updated the link for creating a free Azure account to a more relevant page, improving the user experience for new users.

The documentation for Virtual Machine node pools has been updated to clarify that users can now utilize multi-SKU autoscaling, allowing up to five same-family VM SKUs in a single node pool. Additionally, when creating a new node pool, users can include multiple scale profiles, with at least one being a manual or auto scale profile. The requirements for enabling the cluster autoscaler have also been revised to reflect these changes.
Updated the link for creating a free Azure account to a more relevant page, improving clarity for users without an Azure subscription.
Changed the link for creating a free Azure account to a more suitable page, providing better guidance for users.

The documentation now clarifies that the Application Gateway for Containers supports mutual TLS (mTLS) for backend targets, enhancing security for users implementing this feature. Additionally, the Static IP address configuration is now available for the Application Routing addon, allowing users to assign a static IP address more effectively. These updates improve the overall functionality and security options for users working with ingress solutions in Azure Kubernetes Service.

The documentation now clarifies that the application deployment manifest and ingress deployment both declare and mount the provider volume, ensuring that the TLS certificate is only available when the application or ingress controller is deployed. Additionally, users can now bind the certificate to both the application and ingress controller using the `helm install` command, referencing the Secrets Store CSI Driver's Azure Key Vault provider.
The Managed Gateway API installation documentation has been updated to remove the preview label and clarify the requirements for installation, including the need for a specific Azure CLI version.

The documentation now includes updated release notes for the ALB Controller, highlighting the addition of version 1.10.28, which includes important security updates. Users can also find details on the hotfix for pod crashes in version 1.10.27 and improvements related to Istio service mesh integration in version 1.10.26. Additionally, the instructions for deploying and upgrading the ALB Controller with Helm have been clarified for better user guidance.
[!IMPORTANT]

The documentation now specifies that when creating a key using the `az keyvault key create` command, users must ensure that the `encrypt` and `decrypt` key operations are enabled. Additionally, the link for creating an Azure account has been updated to direct users to a more relevant page regarding pricing and purchase options.

The documentation now clarifies that blue-green upgrades for AKS node pools involve adding new _green_ nodes with updated configurations to the existing node pool, rather than creating a separate _green_ pool. Additionally, it specifies that this strategy can be used for updates beyond just Kubernetes and node image versions, such as certificate rotation and kubelet/OS configuration changes. Users are also reminded that they will incur costs for both _blue_ and _green_ nodes during the upgrade process, and the rollback process has been updated to reflect the new terminology and procedures.

The documentation now emphasizes the recommendation to use Cilium for Kubernetes-native policies, highlighting its advantages over iptables-based solutions. Additionally, there are corrections in the command syntax for creating a pod and applying a policy file, ensuring users have accurate instructions for executing these tasks.

The documentation now includes a new tip in the "Configure a static egress gateway" section, highlighting the option to specify the `--vnet-subnet-id` parameter when creating the gateway node pool. This enhancement allows users to allocate the gateway nodes' private IPs from a custom subnet, improving network isolation and IP address planning for static private IPs used for egress.

The documentation now includes a new section on migrating from self-hosted open-source Karpenter to managed node auto-provisioning (NAP), providing users with detailed steps to enable NAP on their clusters. Users are instructed to ensure they are running the latest version of Karpenter and to avoid deleting the Karpenter CRDs during the migration process, as this could disrupt workloads. Additionally, the steps outline how to uninstall the Helm chart and enable NAP using the `az aks update` command, allowing for a smoother transition to managed node auto-provisioning.

The documentation now includes enhanced details on using member labels to group clusters for update strategies in Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager, allowing for more flexible and dynamic management of cluster updates. Users can configure `memberSelector` at both the stage and group levels, enabling parallel updates with different concurrency limits. Additionally, the note emphasizes the recommendation to use member labels over update groups for better management of large fleets.
Updated the link for creating a free Azure account to a more relevant page, enhancing user guidance for new users.

The documentation now clarifies that virtual networks (VNets) are used for conserving IP address space by employing logically separate Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) ranges for pods. Additionally, it specifies that overlay networking assigns pod IPs from a separate pod CIDR distinct from the node subnet in the VNet, enhancing scalability. Users can also find improved descriptions of the Azure CNI networking models, including their benefits and limitations.

The documentation now clarifies that the Container Network Insights agent is supported in all Azure public regions where AKS is available, rather than just specific regions like centralus and eastus. This change allows users to deploy the agent in a broader range of Azure regions, enhancing flexibility in their configurations. Additionally, the command examples have been updated to reflect this expanded support.
The documentation has been updated to include a note about the `cachingmode` parameter for the target CSI StorageClass, providing guidance on how to include this value in static PV volume attributes.

The documentation now specifies that the "Ubuntu with containerd and CVM" node image is supported for Ubuntu 20.04 and Ubuntu 24.04, while it is not supported for Ubuntu 22.04. This change clarifies the compatibility of the node images with different Ubuntu versions for users utilizing Confidential VMs.
In this article, you learn how to create and manage storage resources for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters in the Azure portal.

The documentation now clarifies that AKS supports AMD-based Confidential VMs utilizing AMD SEV-SNP technology, which offers a hardware-based Trusted Execution Environment without requiring code changes. Additionally, it explicitly states that Intel TDX-based Confidential VMs are not currently supported on AKS, providing users with clearer guidance on available options. The default Ubuntu versions for different Kubernetes versions have also been updated for accuracy.
Updated the Azure subscription link to the pricing options page, providing clearer instructions for users.
Updated the link for creating a free Azure account to a more relevant page, improving guidance for users setting up their Azure subscription.

The document outlines the steps to configure container network metrics filtering in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). It specifies the minimum required versions of Azure CLI and the aks-preview extension, along with the registration of the AdvancedNetworkingDynamicMetricsPreview feature flag.

The document outlines the process of deploying workloads across multiple member clusters using Fleet Manager's resource placement capabilities. It includes updates to the deployment process and clarifies the use of Kubernetes configurations.

This document introduces a new feature in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) that allows cluster operators to evict sensitive pods before freeze events occur on underlying virtual machines. It details the configuration process for both cluster-level and pod-level settings, including the use of a ConfigMap and specific pod labels for eviction. The document also outlines prerequisites, troubleshooting steps, and related content for effective implementation.
Changed the Azure subscription link to the pricing options page, providing clearer instructions for users.

The documentation now clarifies that FQDN filtering is not supported with node-local DNS, which may impact users relying on this configuration. Additionally, it specifies that other Layer 7 policies are also not supported, providing clearer guidance on the limitations of current features.

The documentation now clarifies that the container network insights agent is only supported in Azure public regions, explicitly stating that it is not available in Azure Government, Microsoft Azure operated by 21Vianet, or other sovereign clouds. Additionally, users are instructed to verify that their cluster is in an Azure public region rather than just listing specific regions.

The documentation for creating an internal load balancer in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to clarify that users should now add the `LoadBalancerIP` property to the load balancer YAML manifest, which is being deprecated in favor of using service annotations. Additionally, the command examples have been standardized to use `bash` instead of `azurecli-interactive`, enhancing clarity for users executing commands. Users are also reminded that the AKS cluster identity needs permission to manage network resources when using existing subnets or resource groups.
Updated the Azure subscription link to the pricing options page, enhancing clarity for users.

The documentation now includes a note that not all VM SKUs are accepted by AKS when creating node pools in specific locations, and that node pool creation will fail if the selected SKU doesn't support the requested availability zones. Users can check the availability of VM SKUs by using the `az aks list-vm-skus` command, with an example provided for clarity.

The documentation now includes guidance for users planning to migrate workloads from AWS to Azure, directing them to a new section that outlines example migration scenarios. This addition helps users better understand how to approach their specific use cases when transitioning from Amazon EKS to Azure.

The documentation has been updated to reflect a change in the URL for the Grafana dashboard configuration for the vLLM project, now directing users to the observability section instead of the online serving section. This change ensures that users can access the correct resources for monitoring inference workloads using the `grafana.json` file.

The documentation now specifies that Kubernetes Services can be made globally available on the cross-cluster network only when both the Namespace and the Service have specific annotations set to true. Additionally, it clarifies that Fleet Manager's managed Cilium installation requires explicit opt-in for each Namespace to improve scalability, which differs from the default behavior in Cilium. Users can also temporarily remove a Service from a load-balanced global service by adjusting the relevant annotations on the Service or Namespace.

The documentation has been updated to clarify the process of selecting clusters for update strategies, now emphasizing the use of member labels as the recommended method for flexibility. Users can apply labels to fleet members using the `az fleet member create` and `az fleet member update` commands, allowing for more precise control over cluster selection in update strategies. Additionally, the structure for creating an update strategy has been enhanced with detailed examples, including the use of `maxConcurrency` settings to manage upgrade speeds across clusters.
Changed the Azure subscription link to the pricing options page, improving user guidance.

The documentation now specifies that starting May 2025, AKS will dynamically select the default VM SKU and size based on available capacity and quota if the parameter is left blank during deployment. Additionally, the term "system node pool" is now emphasized in the context of hosting critical system pods, and the documentation clarifies that deploying applications to the `kube-system` namespace is not recommended, with a reference to an FAQ for exceptional cases.

The documentation now includes a new section for users planning to migrate workloads from AWS to Azure, directing them to the "Migrate compute from Amazon Web Services to Azure" guide, which features example migration scenarios. This addition helps users better understand their options and align their migration strategies with specific use cases.
Updated the link for creating a free Azure account to a more relevant page, enhancing user guidance for new Azure users.

Starting in September 2025, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) will implement a quota system for all customers, limiting the maximum number of managed clusters that can be created per region. Existing customers will receive a default limit based on their current usage, while new subscriptions will also have a default limit. Users will be able to view their quota limits and usage in the Azure portal and request additional quota, but enforcement of these limits will only begin after the rollout is complete.

The documentation for automatic managed system node pools in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to reflect changes in the supported regions. Specifically, the region `australiacentral` has been removed from the list of available regions for node pools, which may impact users who were planning to deploy in that region. Users should review the updated list to ensure their deployments align with the current supported regions.
Learn about cross-cluster networking use cases for Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager (Fleet), including high availability, shared services, and multi-cluster observability across AKS clusters.

The documentation now includes a new section on the "Managed Resource Lock Check," which informs users about checks for resource locks on the managed cluster resource group. Additionally, the command for specifying the upgrade override date has been updated to use a placeholder format, and the parameter `--undrainable-node-behavior` is now explicitly mentioned to help users avoid upgrade blockers.

The documentation now includes new sections that explain how to control the order of cluster updates using member labels and update groups, emphasizing that member labels provide greater flexibility for selecting fleet members. Additionally, it clarifies that when a `memberSelector` is set at the stage level, all matching clusters form a single implicit group, and it specifies that the group name is only for display purposes when a `memberSelector` is defined at the group level. Users can now better understand how to manage cluster updates and concurrency settings effectively.

A new article has been added that explains how to list available VM SKUs for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters in a specific Azure region. It includes commands for filtering SKUs and prerequisites for using the Azure CLI.

The documentation now clarifies that the AKS-ACR integration via the `az aks --attach-acr` command is not supported for ABAC-enabled ACR registries, which require the `Container Registry Repository Reader` role instead of the `AcrPull` role. Additionally, users are advised to pre-create a user-assigned identity to avoid latency issues when granting the `AcrPull` role to Microsoft Entra groups. The article also includes updated commands and examples for both Azure CLI and Terraform, ensuring users can effectively integrate AKS with ACR.

The updates to the node auto-drain documentation include new information on handling freeze events, emphasizing the need for opt-in configuration for pod eviction during such events. It also clarifies the actions taken during various scheduled events, including freeze and terminate, and provides links to related features and configurations.

The documentation now specifies that the `microsoft.containernetworkingagent` AKS extension is available in all Azure public regions where AKS is supported, expanding user awareness of its availability. Additionally, it clarifies that the extension is not available in Azure Government, Microsoft Azure operated by 21Vianet, or other sovereign clouds, which may impact deployment decisions for users in those environments.

The documentation now highlights that Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) supports fully managed GPU nodes in preview, which automatically installs and maintains the NVIDIA GPU driver, device plugin, and Data Center GPU Manager metrics exporter. Users can create a managed GPU node pool in a single step by using the `--enable-managed-gpu=true` flag, simplifying the process compared to previous manual installations. Additionally, new parameters `managementMode` and `migStrategy` allow users to control the installation of the GPU stack and configure Multi-Instance GPU strategies during node pool creation.

The documentation for container network logs in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to reflect significant changes in component naming for improved clarity. Users will need to update their Azure CLI and the preview CLI extension, disable and re-enable monitoring, and apply new naming conventions for the `ContainerNetworkLog` custom resource definition (CRD) and associated commands. Additionally, the documentation now emphasizes the importance of using Azure Monitor for log storage to ensure long-term retention and advanced analytics capabilities.

The documentation for AKS node pool upgrades has been updated to include new fallback strategies for maxSurge and maxUnavailable settings. It clarifies that if scaling to the max surge value isn't possible, AKS will attempt a surge of 1 node as a fallback, particularly for agent pools with Kubernetes version >= 1.35. Additionally, the default value for maxUnavailable is now specified as 0, which requires maxSurge to be greater than 0.
The document has been updated to reflect the latest date and includes a note that it applies to Fleet Manager with hub cluster, enhancing clarity for users.

The documentation now includes detailed guidance on configuring User Defined Routes (UDRs) for the Application Gateway for Containers, allowing users to manage routing more effectively between the gateway and AKS clusters. Users can disable Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) route propagation and direct traffic to the internet using specific UDR configurations, which helps prevent issues with client return traffic. Additionally, Network Security Groups (NSGs) are now fully supported for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, allowing for more granular control over inbound and outbound traffic rules.

The documentation now specifies that after creating the federated credential for the `keda-operator` ServiceAccount, users must annotate the ServiceAccount with the managed identity's client ID to bind the identity to the pods. Additionally, a new command example for annotating the ServiceAccount has been added, providing clearer guidance on this process. Users will benefit from this enhanced clarity on the steps required to properly configure Workload Identity for the `keda-operator`.

The documentation for using system node pools in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to clarify that a production AKS cluster with a single system node pool should ideally have at least three nodes for better fault tolerance. Additionally, users are now informed that they need Azure CLI version 2.31 or later installed and configured, and the Terraform configuration examples have been enhanced to include detailed steps for creating and managing both system and user node pools, including specific commands and configurations.

The documentation now specifies that Azure CLI version 2.48.1 or later is required to assign IP addresses from an overlay network or a virtual network, and version 2.69.0 or later is necessary for assigning IP addresses from the node subnet. Additionally, it clarifies that Local Redirect Policy (LRP) is supported from Kubernetes v1.29 and above, and provides updated YAML examples for configuring Cilium Network Policies, including how to allow pod egress to LocalDNS IP addresses.

The documentation for the Azure Key Vault provider for the Secrets Store CSI Driver in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) has been updated to clarify the steps for creating and updating AKS clusters with Azure Key Vault support. Users are also advised to restart their deployments after enabling Istio CNI to ensure pods pick up the changes.

The documentation now includes critical updates regarding security vulnerabilities in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). Specifically, the Azure Monitor Container Insights add-on is noted to require the `nodes/proxy` permission in Kubernetes versions earlier than 1.33, posing a remote code execution risk; users are advised to upgrade to AKS v1.33 or later to mitigate this risk. Additionally, a local privilege escalation vulnerability affecting the Linux kernel's `algif_aead` module has been documented, with a recommendation for users to monitor the rollout of a mitigation that prevents the module from auto-loading on affected AKS nodes.

The documentation on blue-green upgrades has been revised to clarify the process of adding new green nodes with updated configurations to the existing node pool while keeping the original blue nodes running. This change improves understanding of the upgrade process and its implications.

The documentation has been updated to clarify the process of selecting clusters for update strategies, now emphasizing the use of member labels as the recommended approach for greater flexibility. Users can apply labels to fleet members using the `az fleet member create` and `az fleet member update` commands, allowing for more precise control over cluster selection in update strategies. Additionally, the structure for creating update strategies has been enhanced with detailed examples, including the use of `maxConcurrency` settings to manage upgrade speeds across clusters.

The documentation has been updated to reflect the new version 1.10.27 for the ALB Controller installation commands. The documentation now includes a crucial step for users to annotate the `keda-operator` ServiceAccount with the managed identity's client ID after creating the federated credential. Additionally, the link for creating a free Azure account has been updated to direct users to a more relevant page.

The documentation now includes enhanced details on the use of member labels for grouping clusters in update strategies, allowing users to configure their update sequences with Kubernetes-style label selectors. Users can assign multiple labels to fleet members and utilize them for various strategies, rather than being limited to a single update group per member. Additionally, the introduction of the `memberSelector` parameter at both the stage and group levels provides more flexibility in selecting members for updates, improving management of large fleets with dynamic membership.

The documentation now includes a new section on "Capacity Based Surge" (preview), which allows users to set both MaxUnavailable and MaxSurge values based on available capacity. If the MaxSurge value cannot be met due to quota or capacity limitations, AKS will automatically attempt a surge of 1 node as a backup. Additionally, it is now recommended to use the max unavailable configuration for in-place upgrades when additional quota or capacity for the upgrade surge is not possible.

The documentation now includes new sections that explain how to control the order of cluster updates in an update run using member labels and update groups. Users can learn that member labels provide more flexibility for selecting fleet members and that defining groups is optional when a member selector is set at the stage level. Additionally, it clarifies that if a member selector is set at the group level, it overrides the group name for cluster selection purposes.

The documentation now specifies that users must install Azure CLI version 2.86.0 or later to enable Istio CNI for the Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). Additionally, users can enable Istio CNI during the service mesh add-on installation by using the `--proxy-redirection-mechanism CNIChaining` parameter, and existing users can switch to Istio CNI with the command `az aks mesh proxy-redirection-mechanism --mechanism CNIChaining`. It is also noted that existing pods will not automatically switch to the `istio-validation` init container, requiring users to restart their deployments after enabling Istio CNI.

The documentation now clarifies that when the Application Gateway for Containers add-on is enabled on an AKS cluster using the AKS-managed virtual network option, a subnet named `aks-appgateway` is automatically created with delegation enabled for `Microsoft.ServiceNetworking/TrafficController`. Additionally, users can verify that the GatewayClass has a condition labeled **Valid GatewayClass**, indicating that a default GatewayClass is set up and managed by the ALB Controller.

The documentation now includes a new section on Network Security Groups (NSGs) for the Application Gateway for Containers, detailing their support on association subnets. Users can configure both inbound and outbound NSG rules for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, while those created before this date will always allow inbound traffic on ports 80 and 443, regardless of configured rules. Additionally, the implications of "Deny all" rules are clarified, highlighting how they can block traffic unless explicit allow exceptions are defined.

The release notes for the ALB Controller have been updated to reflect the latest version changes, including fixes for pod crashes and security patches. The documentation now specifies that updates will automatically be applied to the cluster when using the AKS add-on.

The documentation now clarifies that the ALB Controller Service Mesh Extension requires the use of the `istio.io/rev` label for mutual authentication in addition to the existing `istio-injection: enabled` label. Users can also obtain the Istio version using specific commands in Kubectl or Azure CLI, which aids in configuring sidecar injection correctly. Additionally, the version of the ALB Controller has been updated to 1.10.26, ensuring users are aware of the latest version for deployment.

This document provides a comprehensive guide on how to view available VM SKUs for Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters in a specific Azure region. It includes commands for listing SKUs, filtering options, and prerequisites for using the Azure CLI and aks-preview extension.

The documentation now reflects that the ALB Controller Service Mesh Extension is updated to version 1.10.26, enhancing its functionality. Users are informed that the integration assumes an Istio sidecar-based service mesh, as ambient mode is not supported. Additionally, the instructions for configuring sidecar injection have been clarified, specifying the use of the `istio.io/rev` label for the Istio AKS add-on, which differs from the open-source Istio's `istio-injection=enabled` label.

The release notes for the ALB Controller detail updates and fixes, including a hotfix for a pod crash and security patches. It also includes changes to the release date and version updates, ensuring users are informed about the latest improvements.

The documentation now includes updated tables for `karpenter.azure.com` SKU selectors and `kubernetes.io` well-known labels that can be used in the `spec.requirements` section of the `NodePool` API. Users can now specify additional parameters such as `karpenter.azure.com/sku-series` for VM SKU series and `kubernetes.azure.com/cluster` for the AKS cluster name, enhancing their ability to define VM characteristics and node specifications more precisely.

The documentation now includes enhanced details on the use of `memberSelector` for grouping clusters in update strategies, allowing users to utilize Kubernetes-style label selectors for more flexible and multi-dimensional member selection. Users can configure `memberSelector` at both the stage and group levels, enabling them to manage large fleets with dynamic membership more effectively. Additionally, a new section on grouping clusters using member labels has been added, providing guidance on how to implement this feature in update strategies.

This document highlights the importance of node image versions in Azure Kubernetes Fleet Manager, noting that they are only valid for 90 days from their publish date. It emphasizes the need for timely upgrades to avoid potential failures during cluster updates.

The documentation has been updated to reflect a change in the URL for the vLLM project's Grafana dashboard configuration. Users should now refer to the new link under the "observability" section for accessing the `grafana.json` file, which is essential for inference workload monitoring. This change ensures users are directed to the correct resources for setting up their monitoring dashboards.

The documentation now clarifies that if Advanced Container Networking Services (ACNS) security is disabled, FQDN and L7 policies (HTTP(s), Kafka, and gRPC) are blocked. Additionally, it highlights that Alpine-based container images may face DNS resolution issues with Cilium Network Policies due to musl libc's limitations, and users are advised to explicitly define all search domains in the Network Policy's DNS rules using wildcard patterns.
Learn how to set up a compatible Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster for AKS desktop.
This document has been modified to improve the integration instructions for Microsoft Sentinel workspace, ensuring users have the correct links and guidance for enabling the workspace effectively.

The documentation now specifies that for associations created on or after April 23, 2026, Network Security Groups (NSGs) are fully supported on the Application Gateway for Containers association subnet, allowing both inbound and outbound rules. For associations created before this date, while inbound NSG rules can be configured, traffic on ports 80 and 443 will always be allowed, which may impact access to the frontend if "Deny all" rules are applied without explicit allow exceptions. Users should be aware of these changes to effectively manage their network security configurations.

This document provides a guide on deploying the MetalLB extension for Azure Arc-enabled Kubernetes clusters using Azure CLI. It includes prerequisites and detailed steps for setting up a load balancer.

The documentation now clarifies that when the Application Gateway for Containers add-on is enabled on an AKS cluster using the AKS-managed virtual network option, a subnet named `aks-appgateway` is automatically created with delegation enabled for `Microsoft.ServiceNetworking/TrafficController`. Additionally, users will see a condition labeled **Valid GatewayClass** in the GatewayClass status, indicating that a default GatewayClass is set up and managed by the ALB Controller.
Learn how to use the AI-powered troubleshooting assistant in AKS desktop to diagnose and resolve Kubernetes issues using natural language.

The documentation now includes a critical note stating that node image versions are only valid for 90 days from their original publish date, which is essential for users to avoid potential upgrade failures for member clusters. Additionally, the guide has been updated to clarify that the generated update run is not automatically started, allowing users to review it before proceeding. Users can also utilize the `az fleet autoupgradeprofile generate-update-run` command to generate new update runs based on the current AKS-published Kubernetes or node image version.

The documentation for AKS reserved labels has been updated to clarify the usage of certain labels, including the introduction of a new label, `kubernetes.azure.com/priority`, which replaces the deprecated `kubernetes.azure.com/scalesetpriority`. Users can now refer to the updated table for recommended substitutes for deprecated labels, enhancing clarity on which labels to use for their configurations. Additionally, the formatting of example outputs has been improved for better readability.

The LocalDNS configuration documentation has been modified, removing redundant information about the default configuration applied by AKS. This change streamlines the content, making it clearer for users who may not specify a custom configuration file.
